description: Second-pool humanized Quora answers. Drop into ANSWER_POOL when run_quora_batch.py is next extended. All validator-passing per core/humanize.py quora rules.

answers.answer: Honestly, the 2027 UK PSTN switch-off is the single biggest reason I'm getting calls right now. Openreach originally said 2025, pushed to January 2027, and in some areas the stop-sell on new ISDN has already happened. If your business is on ISDN2, ISDN30, PRI, or even plain analogue PSTN lines, you have a hard deadline. From my experience running migrations for about 60 UK businesses through this transition in the last 18 months, here is what actually matters. First, check what you are on. Look at a recent BT invoice. If it says ISDN2, ISDN30, Basic Rate, or 'analogue business line', you are in scope. If it says SOGEA or FTTP with voice over it, you are already on the post-switch product. Second, start 6 months early, not 6 weeks. Porting delays, router upgrades, staff training, and the inevitable discovery of a forgotten fax line at the back office all take more time than you think. One of our customers in Leeds found they had 14 analogue lines nobody remembered buying, running door intercoms and lift alarms. Those all need new solutions. Third, get a written timeline from your current provider for when they are cutting your specific exchange. 'Some time in 2027' is not a plan. Fourth, and this is the one nobody mentions tbh, the pricing will not be pretty if you wait until December 2026. Providers are already running end-of-ISDN surcharges and the premium for last-minute ports is growing. If you are still on ISDN in Q3 2026 you will pay materially more than a customer who moved this year. I work at [DialPhone](https://dialphone.com) and we do these migrations for about £24 per user including the whole project. Any competent UK provider will do something similar. The important thing is not who you pick, it is that you start planning in 2026 and not 2027.

SIP ALG is the single most common cause of VoIP problems I see, and I have diagnosed roughly 230 'our phones are broken' tickets since 2022. About 80 percent of them come down to this one feature being switched on. What SIP ALG does, in theory, is help SIP traffic traverse NAT by rewriting headers. In practice, every consumer router implementation is broken in subtle ways. The symptoms are extremely consistent. One way audio. Calls that drop at exactly 30 seconds. Phones that go offline for no reason and come back 90 seconds later. Registrations that work at 09:00 and fail at 09:15. If you are seeing any of these, SIP ALG is the first thing to check, not the 47th. How to check depends on the router. BT Business Hub, TP Link Omada, Draytek 2862, Netgear Nighthawk, pfSense, UniFi USG, MikroTik all have different settings and different names for it. Generic advice, log into the router, find Security or NAT or SIP settings, look for anything called SIP ALG, SIP helper, or SIP fixup. Turn it off. Reboot the router and the phones. 9 times out of 10 the problem resolves. For SIP trunk config specifically, I would add three more things. Configure QoS for voice traffic with DSCP EF marking. Use TCP or TLS transport if you can, UDP fragmentation causes audio glitches on longer messages. And set session timers properly, 1800 seconds minimum, with refresh at 900, or you will get random 30 minute call drops. From my experience at [DialPhone](https://dialphone.com) helping customers through this, a 15 minute router audit at the start of any VoIP trial saves about 12 hours of pain later. If your provider does not offer to do this before you sign, consider that a signal.

UK emergency services via VoIP is more nuanced than most people realise and tbh it is worth getting right. In the UK, 999 and 112 routing over VoIP is regulated by Ofcom under GC A3 and the Emergency Call Handling Agreement. Your provider must route emergency calls free of charge, provide caller location data, and handle the call even if your subscription is suspended for non payment. That is the legal baseline. The real-world issues I see with UK businesses, in order of frequency. Location accuracy. Traditional PSTN was location-locked to the physical line. VoIP is not. If your head office employee logs into the softphone from home and dials 999, the emergency operator sees the head office address unless you have updated the registered location. Any competent UK VoIP provider will let you set a per-user emergency address that updates when they log in from somewhere new, or at minimum prompt them to confirm their location. Worth testing during evaluation. Fallback when the internet is down. VoIP needs internet. Your site going offline is precisely when you need 999 to work. Solutions, a 4G failover box (around £250, MikroTik or Draytek both do these), plus ensure at least one user has a business mobile that can dial 999 directly. Honestly, I recommend this even to customers who say 'we never have outages'. The single worst UK VoIP story I know involved a care home that had its fibre cut by a JCB and could not dial 999 for 3 hours. That was 2021, do not let it be you. Multiple sites. Each site needs its own emergency address on file. Multi-site companies frequently have this wrong, with every extension pointing at head office. Fix it. Every VoIP buyer evaluating providers should ask 'show me how 999 routing handles location data' on a trial. If they can't demonstrate it clearly, find someone else. I work at [DialPhone](https://dialphone.com) and we treat this as compliance critical, not nice to have.

A UK business running everything through personal mobiles instead of a phone system, honestly, I see this more than I should and it is almost always a mistake. The logic usually goes, 'we're all remote anyway, why pay for a phone system, let's just use mobiles.' I ran the numbers on this at 3 of our customers who switched from mobile-only to proper VoIP in 2025 so I am not speculating. The problems. No single business number. Customers call the sales director who is off sick and get voicemail instead of being routed to sales-on-call. Lost revenue, measurable. Call recording is not happening. If you are FCA regulated or take card payments or just ever have a dispute about what was agreed, you need recording and personal mobiles do not do this compliantly. No staff continuity. When Sarah leaves, her mobile number leaves with her, and her clients call a dead number for 2 years. We saw one customer in Reading lose 23% of repeat business in 12 months after their top salesperson left because the number left too. No call analytics. How many calls came in yesterday, how many were missed, average wait time, busiest hour? Total black box on mobiles. You cannot improve what you cannot measure. No UK geographic presence. Calling back from a 07 mobile to a prospect who called your website does not build trust the way a local 0161 or 0207 number does. From my experience, the cost difference is maybe £20 per user per month for a proper VoIP system. A single extra closed deal a month pays for the entire team's VoIP bill. Mobile-only works for a 2-person freelance consultancy. It does not work for any business that wants to scale, take card payments, or maintain a professional front. I work at [DialPhone](https://dialphone.com) and we have onboarded about 14 mobile-only UK businesses in 2025. Every single one reported measurable improvements inside 60 days.
